← In Search of the Perfect 6-Player Risk Board

What changed · 2026-06-21 An earlier version of this research crowned a 10-country board scoring ~87. That board was un-buildable and its score was inflated. The generator added cross-country attack links with no per-territory limits, so a territory could be attackable from 5+ different countries (a cross-map "hub") and from 7 different territories — neither is a real Risk square, and the hubs let nobody fortress up, which manufactured contestability and pumped the score. We added two hard generator caps — a territory borders at most 4 distinct countries and is attackable from at most 5 territories (real Risk's max is 6) — which voided the old winners (−~17 composite) and forced a fresh search over hundreds of buildable boards. The maps below are the honest, drawable result. (Full story: research-paper.md Iterations 14–15.)

risk-lab · final maps — 10 best 8-country vs 10 best 9-country boards

The 20 strongest buildable boards (cross-country adjacency capped — no attack-from-everywhere hubs), ranked under the realistic opening-robust objective at 500 games with 95% bootstrap CIs. World/Classic shown as the bars. 7 of the 20 beat World Risk with confidence.

How to read the fun columns

Composite is the 0–100 balance+fun score; Δ is realistic − all-war (small = the board doesn't lean on the random deal). The rest are the texture of a fun game: Progress = how much winning runs through capturing countries (a live bonus economy, not a grind); Snowball = how often the early leader just wins (lower is better); Comeback = how often a trailing player still wins (higher is better); Median rds = game length; Persona-fair = how evenly play-styles win (lower is fairer); maxAdj = the buildability cap (distinct countries any one territory borders — all ≤4).

#MapctCompositeΔ ProgressSnowComebackMedian rdsPersona-fairmaxAdj Fun signals

—Standard Risk World real Risk board—c76.2[72–78]-14.10.8547%21%320.1832
—Classic real Risk board—c75.5[71–77]-11.90.8546%16%320.1952

The fun read (my pick)

Composite ranks balance+fun together, but if you optimise purely for a fun table — a lead that can be overturned, real comebacks, and a bonus economy worth racing for — these stand out:

  • Gen 43t/9c #646721 (9c) — composite 84.7, snowball 31%, comeback 35%, progression 1.12. live economy · lead is losable · healthy length.
  • Gen 45t/9c #682232 (9c) — composite 87.0, snowball 31%, comeback 40%, progression 0.99. live economy · lead is losable · real comebacks · healthy length.
  • Gen 44t/8c #714901 (8c) — composite 81.4, snowball 35%, comeback 31%, progression 1.04. live economy · lead is losable · healthy length.

Best 8-country map overall: Gen 42t/8c #58444 (86.3). Best 9-country map: Gen 45t/9c #682232 (87.0) — your preferred setup, within -0.6 of the best 8c. Both saved to maps/ritan_final_8c.json / ritan_final_9c.json.

Per-map breakdown

full breakdown · Wilson CIs + leader-penalty curve · the raw board JSON is at the bottom of each · 300 games

risk-lab · 9c/45t #682232

45 territories · 9 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

33median rounds 100%decisive 29%snowball 42%comeback 0.017seat fairness 0.070persona fairness 1.0progression

Balance + fun score

84.9composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness83
persona fairness42
decisiveness100
length100
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

64%games with a swarm 98%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 98% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Turn order is fair (seat win-rate std-dev 0.017).
  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 33 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 29%, comeback 42%, 6.4 lead changes/game).
  • Country 2 is the easiest hold (bonus/border 1.50) but not dominant (9% of winners).
  • 2 chokepoint territories create defensible fronts.

Weaknesses

  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.070); win rates: balanced 14%, countryal 21%, expander 17%, opportunist 8%, turtle 11%, warlord 29%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.070): the 'low-contention' opening wins 29% (fair share 17%).
  • Country 1 looks under-rewarded (bonus 1 for 3 borders; held by 11% of winners).

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 11330.3311%
Country 23821.509%
Country 31320.5014%
Country 41330.3314%
Country 52630.679%
Country 641131.334%
Country 71330.3318%
Country 81330.3316%
Country 92550.405%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 3–11 (spread 8) · bonuses 1–4 · most defensible: Country 2 (3 bonus for 2 borders = 1.5 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
warlord29% [24.2–34.4] n=300
countryal21% [17.1–26.3] n=300
expander17% [12.9–21.3] n=300
balanced14% [10.8–18.8] n=300
turtle11% [7.9–15.0] n=300
opportunist8% [5.2–11.2] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.070.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 016% [12.0–20.2] n=300
seat 116% [12.6–20.9] n=300
seat 218% [13.8–22.4] n=300
seat 318% [13.8–22.4] n=300
seat 419% [15.0–23.8] n=300
seat 514% [10.2–18.0] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.017. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
low-contention29% [24.2–34.4] n=300
small-country21% [17.1–26.3] n=300
random17% [12.9–21.3] n=300
blocker14% [10.8–18.8] n=300
concentrate11% [7.9–15.0] n=300
spread8% [5.2–11.2] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.070 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 5avg truce length (rounds) 99%truces betrayed 14%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 14% vs. a 17% fair share = a -2% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Structure

76edges 3.4avg degree 2–5degree range 2chokepoints 11diameter 51.6country flips/game

Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · 9c/49t #505559

49 territories · 9 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

28median rounds 100%decisive 32%snowball 34%comeback 0.027seat fairness 0.050persona fairness 1.2progression

Balance + fun score

84.9composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness73
persona fairness59
decisiveness100
length93
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

56%games with a swarm 96%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 96% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Turn order is fair (seat win-rate std-dev 0.027).
  • All play styles stay viable (persona fairness 0.050).
  • Board is opening-agnostic (setup fairness 0.050) — no single way of claiming/stacking dominates.
  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 28 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 32%, comeback 34%, 4.5 lead changes/game).
  • Country 5 is the easiest hold (bonus/border 2.00) but not dominant (5% of winners).
  • 3 chokepoint territories create defensible fronts.

Weaknesses

  • Country 6 looks under-rewarded (bonus 1 for 3 borders; held by 10% of winners).

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 11320.5023%
Country 24931.333%
Country 34931.338%
Country 43631.0012%
Country 541022.005%
Country 61330.3310%
Country 71330.339%
Country 81330.3311%
Country 91320.5036%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 3–10 (spread 7) · bonuses 1–4 · most defensible: Country 5 (4 bonus for 2 borders = 2.0 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
warlord24% [19.5–29.1] n=300
countryal20% [16.2–25.2] n=300
expander19% [15.0–23.8] n=300
turtle15% [11.7–19.8] n=300
balanced11% [8.2–15.4] n=300
opportunist10% [7.1–13.9] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.050.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 013% [9.9–17.6] n=300
seat 118% [14.4–23.1] n=300
seat 214% [10.8–18.8] n=300
seat 321% [17.1–26.3] n=300
seat 418% [13.8–22.4] n=300
seat 515% [11.4–19.5] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.027. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
low-contention24% [19.5–29.1] n=300
small-country20% [16.2–25.2] n=300
random19% [15.0–23.8] n=300
concentrate15% [11.7–19.8] n=300
blocker11% [8.2–15.4] n=300
spread10% [7.1–13.9] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.050 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 5avg truce length (rounds) 99%truces betrayed 20%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 20% vs. a 17% fair share = a +3% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Structure

77edges 3.1avg degree 2–5degree range 3chokepoints 10diameter 39.5country flips/game

Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · 8c/42t #58444

42 territories · 8 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

39median rounds 100%decisive 28%snowball 37%comeback 0.026seat fairness 0.078persona fairness 0.7progression

Balance + fun score

81.9composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness74
persona fairness35
decisiveness100
length100
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

66%games with a swarm 92%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 92% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Turn order is fair (seat win-rate std-dev 0.026).
  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 39 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 28%, comeback 37%, 7.8 lead changes/game).
  • Country 2 is the easiest hold (bonus/border 1.67) but not dominant (2% of winners).

Weaknesses

  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.078); win rates: balanced 17%, countryal 16%, expander 15%, opportunist 8%, turtle 11%, warlord 33%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.078): the 'low-contention' opening wins 33% (fair share 17%).
  • Country 1 looks under-rewarded (bonus 1 for 3 borders; held by 15% of winners).

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 11330.3315%
Country 251231.672%
Country 33640.756%
Country 42540.505%
Country 52530.6711%
Country 62530.6710%
Country 71330.338%
Country 81330.3310%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 3–12 (spread 9) · bonuses 1–5 · most defensible: Country 2 (5 bonus for 3 borders = 1.7 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
warlord33% [27.6–38.2] n=300
balanced17% [12.9–21.3] n=300
countryal16% [12.6–20.9] n=300
expander15% [11.4–19.5] n=300
turtle11% [8.2–15.4] n=300
opportunist8% [5.4–11.6] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.078.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 012% [8.8–16.2] n=300
seat 115% [11.4–19.5] n=300
seat 219% [14.7–23.5] n=300
seat 318% [14.1–22.7] n=300
seat 417% [12.9–21.3] n=300
seat 520% [15.6–24.5] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.026. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
low-contention33% [27.6–38.2] n=300
blocker17% [12.9–21.3] n=300
small-country16% [12.6–20.9] n=300
random15% [11.4–19.5] n=300
concentrate11% [8.2–15.4] n=300
spread8% [5.4–11.6] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.078 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 5avg truce length (rounds) 100%truces betrayed 16%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 16% vs. a 17% fair share = a -1% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

73edges 3.5avg degree 3–5degree range 0chokepoints 8diameter 48.2country flips/game

Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · 8c/40t #607126

40 territories · 8 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

41median rounds 100%decisive 28%snowball 37%comeback 0.028seat fairness 0.061persona fairness 0.9progression

Balance + fun score

84.2composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness72
persona fairness49
decisiveness100
length100
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

67%games with a swarm 92%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 92% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Turn order is fair (seat win-rate std-dev 0.028).
  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 41 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 28%, comeback 37%, 8.2 lead changes/game).
  • Country 3 is the easiest hold (bonus/border 1.33) but not dominant (1% of winners).

Weaknesses

  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.061); win rates: balanced 14%, countryal 19%, expander 14%, opportunist 11%, turtle 13%, warlord 29%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.061): the 'low-contention' opening wins 29% (fair share 17%).
  • Country 4 looks under-rewarded (bonus 1 for 3 borders; held by 12% of winners).

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 12630.6715%
Country 22440.506%
Country 341131.331%
Country 41330.3312%
Country 51330.3320%
Country 61320.5017%
Country 71330.3315%
Country 83731.003%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 3–11 (spread 8) · bonuses 1–4 · most defensible: Country 3 (4 bonus for 3 borders = 1.3 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
warlord29% [24.5–34.7] n=300
countryal19% [14.7–23.5] n=300
expander14% [10.8–18.8] n=300
balanced14% [10.5–18.4] n=300
turtle13% [9.7–17.3] n=300
opportunist11% [7.7–14.7] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.061.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 015% [11.7–19.8] n=300
seat 120% [15.6–24.5] n=300
seat 216% [12.3–20.6] n=300
seat 313% [9.7–17.3] n=300
seat 415% [11.4–19.5] n=300
seat 521% [16.8–26.0] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.028. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
low-contention29% [24.5–34.7] n=300
small-country19% [14.7–23.5] n=300
random14% [10.8–18.8] n=300
blocker14% [10.5–18.4] n=300
concentrate13% [9.7–17.3] n=300
spread11% [7.7–14.7] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.061 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 4avg truce length (rounds) 100%truces betrayed 13%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 13% vs. a 17% fair share = a -4% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Structure

66edges 3.3avg degree 2–5degree range 0chokepoints 8diameter 54.8country flips/game

Board data (JSON)

Show the raw board definition — countries, bonuses, territories, adjacency

Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · 9c/43t #646721

43 territories · 9 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

29median rounds 100%decisive 31%snowball 35%comeback 0.015seat fairness 0.086persona fairness 1.1progression

Balance + fun score

82.0composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness85
persona fairness28
decisiveness100
length97
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

67%games with a swarm 95%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 95% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Turn order is fair (seat win-rate std-dev 0.015).
  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 29 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 31%, comeback 35%, 6.1 lead changes/game).
  • Country 4 is the easiest hold (bonus/border 2.00) but not dominant (7% of winners).

Weaknesses

  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.086); win rates: balanced 9%, countryal 26%, expander 14%, opportunist 11%, turtle 9%, warlord 31%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.086): the 'low-contention' opening wins 31% (fair share 17%).
  • Country 7 looks under-rewarded (bonus 1 for 3 borders; held by 24% of winners).

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 13721.509%
Country 22421.0013%
Country 32440.507%
Country 441022.007%
Country 52521.0019%
Country 62440.509%
Country 71330.3324%
Country 81330.339%
Country 91330.3310%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 3–10 (spread 7) · bonuses 1–4 · most defensible: Country 4 (4 bonus for 2 borders = 2.0 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
warlord31% [26.0–36.4] n=300
countryal26% [21.1–30.9] n=300
expander14% [10.8–18.8] n=300
opportunist11% [7.9–15.0] n=300
turtle9% [6.5–13.2] n=300
balanced9% [6.0–12.4] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.086.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 014% [10.5–18.4] n=300
seat 118% [13.8–22.4] n=300
seat 218% [13.8–22.4] n=300
seat 316% [12.3–20.6] n=300
seat 416% [12.3–20.6] n=300
seat 519% [14.7–23.5] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.015. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
low-contention31% [26.0–36.4] n=300
small-country26% [21.1–30.9] n=300
random14% [10.8–18.8] n=300
spread11% [7.9–15.0] n=300
concentrate9% [6.5–13.2] n=300
blocker9% [6.0–12.4] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.086 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 5avg truce length (rounds) 99%truces betrayed 14%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 14% vs. a 17% fair share = a -3% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Structure

74edges 3.4avg degree 2–5degree range 0chokepoints 9diameter 48.2country flips/game

Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · 8c/47t #379873

47 territories · 8 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

32median rounds 100%decisive 36%snowball 35%comeback 0.018seat fairness 0.090persona fairness 0.8progression

Balance + fun score

81.4composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness82
persona fairness25
decisiveness100
length100
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

66%games with a swarm 96%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 96% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Turn order is fair (seat win-rate std-dev 0.018).
  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 32 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 36%, comeback 35%, 6.0 lead changes/game).
  • Country 6 is the easiest hold (bonus/border 1.67) but not dominant (2% of winners).

Weaknesses

  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.090); win rates: balanced 10%, countryal 26%, expander 12%, opportunist 7%, turtle 13%, warlord 32%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.090): the 'low-contention' opening wins 32% (fair share 17%).
  • Country 3 looks under-rewarded (bonus 1 for 3 borders; held by 24% of winners).

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 12530.6710%
Country 23631.008%
Country 31330.3324%
Country 41330.339%
Country 53650.602%
Country 651231.672%
Country 74850.803%
Country 82430.6725%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 3–12 (spread 9) · bonuses 1–5 · most defensible: Country 6 (5 bonus for 3 borders = 1.7 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
warlord32% [26.7–37.1] n=300
countryal26% [21.7–31.6] n=300
turtle13% [9.4–16.9] n=300
expander12% [9.1–16.5] n=300
balanced10% [6.8–13.5] n=300
opportunist7% [4.9–10.9] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.090.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 018% [13.8–22.4] n=300
seat 115% [11.7–19.8] n=300
seat 218% [14.4–23.1] n=300
seat 316% [12.6–20.9] n=300
seat 414% [10.2–18.0] n=300
seat 519% [14.7–23.5] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.018. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
low-contention32% [26.7–37.1] n=300
small-country26% [21.7–31.6] n=300
concentrate13% [9.4–16.9] n=300
random12% [9.1–16.5] n=300
blocker10% [6.8–13.5] n=300
spread7% [4.9–10.9] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.090 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 4avg truce length (rounds) 100%truces betrayed 16%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 16% vs. a 17% fair share = a -1% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Structure

75edges 3.2avg degree 2–5degree range 0chokepoints 9diameter 33.5country flips/game

Board data (JSON)

Show the raw board definition — countries, bonuses, territories, adjacency

The canonical vector format (loadable by graph.load_map / the map editor) — copy it to build a real, good-looking map from this board.

Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · 8c/39t #420813

39 territories · 8 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

34median rounds 100%decisive 31%snowball 34%comeback 0.019seat fairness 0.079persona fairness 1.0progression

Balance + fun score

83.0composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness81
persona fairness35
decisiveness100
length100
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

71%games with a swarm 96%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 96% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Turn order is fair (seat win-rate std-dev 0.019).
  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 34 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 31%, comeback 34%, 7.1 lead changes/game).
  • Country 8 is the easiest hold (bonus/border 0.75) but not dominant (9% of winners).

Weaknesses

  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.079); win rates: balanced 11%, countryal 29%, expander 12%, opportunist 14%, turtle 9%, warlord 26%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.079): the 'small-country' opening wins 29% (fair share 17%).
  • Country 2 looks under-rewarded (bonus 1 for 3 borders; held by 13% of winners).

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 12430.6721%
Country 21330.3313%
Country 31330.3315%
Country 43650.606%
Country 51320.5029%
Country 62530.677%
Country 73850.602%
Country 83740.759%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 3–8 (spread 5) · bonuses 1–3 · most defensible: Country 8 (3 bonus for 4 borders = 0.8 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
countryal29% [24.5–34.7] n=300
warlord26% [21.1–30.9] n=300
opportunist14% [10.2–18.0] n=300
expander12% [8.5–15.8] n=300
balanced11% [7.7–14.7] n=300
turtle9% [6.3–12.8] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.079.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 016% [12.3–20.6] n=300
seat 115% [11.1–19.1] n=300
seat 220% [16.2–25.2] n=300
seat 317% [13.5–22.0] n=300
seat 417% [13.2–21.7] n=300
seat 515% [11.1–19.1] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.019. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
small-country29% [24.5–34.7] n=300
low-contention26% [21.1–30.9] n=300
spread14% [10.2–18.0] n=300
random12% [8.5–15.8] n=300
blocker11% [7.7–14.7] n=300
concentrate9% [6.3–12.8] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.079 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 5avg truce length (rounds) 99%truces betrayed 13%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 13% vs. a 17% fair share = a -4% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Structure

65edges 3.3avg degree 2–4degree range 0chokepoints 8diameter 42.6country flips/game

Board data (JSON)

Show the raw board definition — countries, bonuses, territories, adjacency

Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · 8c/41t #162069

41 territories · 8 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

29median rounds 100%decisive 32%snowball 35%comeback 0.030seat fairness 0.081persona fairness 1.0progression

Balance + fun score

79.8composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness70
persona fairness32
decisiveness100
length97
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

64%games with a swarm 97%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 97% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Turn order is fair (seat win-rate std-dev 0.030).
  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 29 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 32%, comeback 35%, 5.6 lead changes/game).
  • Country 7 is the easiest hold (bonus/border 1.33) but not dominant (4% of winners).

Weaknesses

  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.081); win rates: balanced 9%, countryal 23%, expander 13%, opportunist 16%, turtle 8%, warlord 31%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.081): the 'low-contention' opening wins 31% (fair share 17%).
  • Country 5 looks under-rewarded (bonus 1 for 3 borders; held by 24% of winners).

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 12530.679%
Country 22430.6719%
Country 31320.5020%
Country 42440.5016%
Country 51330.3324%
Country 63731.008%
Country 74931.334%
Country 83650.602%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 3–9 (spread 6) · bonuses 1–4 · most defensible: Country 7 (4 bonus for 3 borders = 1.3 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
warlord31% [25.7–36.1] n=300
countryal23% [18.9–28.4] n=300
opportunist16% [12.6–20.9] n=300
expander13% [9.9–17.6] n=300
balanced9% [6.0–12.4] n=300
turtle8% [5.2–11.2] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.081.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 019% [14.7–23.5] n=300
seat 119% [14.7–23.5] n=300
seat 219% [15.3–24.2] n=300
seat 318% [14.4–23.1] n=300
seat 413% [9.9–17.6] n=300
seat 512% [8.5–15.8] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.030. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
low-contention31% [25.7–36.1] n=300
small-country23% [18.9–28.4] n=300
spread16% [12.6–20.9] n=300
random13% [9.9–17.6] n=300
blocker9% [6.0–12.4] n=300
concentrate8% [5.2–11.2] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.081 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 5avg truce length (rounds) 100%truces betrayed 17%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 17% vs. a 17% fair share = a +0% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Structure

76edges 3.7avg degree 2–5degree range 0chokepoints 9diameter 30.5country flips/game

Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · 8c/44t #714901

44 territories · 8 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

32median rounds 100%decisive 34%snowball 31%comeback 0.017seat fairness 0.084persona fairness 1.0progression

Balance + fun score

82.7composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness83
persona fairness30
decisiveness100
length100
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

66%games with a swarm 96%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 96% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Turn order is fair (seat win-rate std-dev 0.017).
  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 32 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 34%, comeback 31%, 6.2 lead changes/game).
  • Country 3 is the easiest hold (bonus/border 1.33) but not dominant (2% of winners).

Weaknesses

  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.084); win rates: balanced 11%, countryal 22%, expander 14%, opportunist 8%, turtle 12%, warlord 33%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.084): the 'low-contention' opening wins 33% (fair share 17%).
  • Country 1 looks under-rewarded (bonus 1 for 3 borders; held by 21% of winners).

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 11330.3321%
Country 21330.3322%
Country 34931.332%
Country 42540.506%
Country 51330.3316%
Country 62430.6727%
Country 73731.005%
Country 841031.334%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 3–10 (spread 7) · bonuses 1–4 · most defensible: Country 3 (4 bonus for 3 borders = 1.3 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
warlord33% [27.6–38.2] n=300
countryal22% [18.0–27.4] n=300
expander14% [10.2–18.0] n=300
turtle12% [8.5–15.8] n=300
balanced11% [8.2–15.4] n=300
opportunist8% [5.7–12.0] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.084.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 014% [10.5–18.4] n=300
seat 116% [12.3–20.6] n=300
seat 216% [12.0–20.2] n=300
seat 319% [15.0–23.8] n=300
seat 418% [14.4–23.1] n=300
seat 517% [13.2–21.7] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.017. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
low-contention33% [27.6–38.2] n=300
small-country22% [18.0–27.4] n=300
random14% [10.2–18.0] n=300
concentrate12% [8.5–15.8] n=300
blocker11% [8.2–15.4] n=300
spread8% [5.7–12.0] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.084 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 4avg truce length (rounds) 100%truces betrayed 15%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 15% vs. a 17% fair share = a -2% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Structure

74edges 3.4avg degree 3–5degree range 0chokepoints 8diameter 38.7country flips/game

Board data (JSON)

Show the raw board definition — countries, bonuses, territories, adjacency

The canonical vector format (loadable by graph.load_map / the map editor) — copy it to build a real, good-looking map from this board.

Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · 9c/46t #244069

46 territories · 9 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

31median rounds 100%decisive 36%snowball 31%comeback 0.024seat fairness 0.093persona fairness 0.9progression

Balance + fun score

79.7composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness76
persona fairness23
decisiveness100
length100
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

69%games with a swarm 99%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 99% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Turn order is fair (seat win-rate std-dev 0.024).
  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 31 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 36%, comeback 31%, 5.6 lead changes/game).
  • Country 2 is the easiest hold (bonus/border 2.00) but not dominant (16% of winners).
  • 2 chokepoint territories create defensible fronts.

Weaknesses

  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.093); win rates: balanced 12%, countryal 28%, expander 14%, opportunist 6%, turtle 10%, warlord 31%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.093): the 'low-contention' opening wins 31% (fair share 17%).
  • Country 3 looks under-rewarded (bonus 1 for 3 borders; held by 18% of winners).

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 12530.674%
Country 22512.0016%
Country 31330.3318%
Country 43621.5015%
Country 541022.008%
Country 61330.338%
Country 71330.3312%
Country 83731.006%
Country 92440.507%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 3–10 (spread 7) · bonuses 1–4 · most defensible: Country 2 (2 bonus for 1 border = 2.0 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
warlord31% [25.7–36.1] n=300
countryal28% [23.2–33.3] n=300
expander14% [10.2–18.0] n=300
balanced12% [8.8–16.2] n=300
turtle10% [6.8–13.5] n=300
opportunist6% [3.8–9.3] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.093.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 017% [13.5–22.0] n=300
seat 115% [11.1–19.1] n=300
seat 213% [9.7–17.3] n=300
seat 320% [15.6–24.5] n=300
seat 416% [12.3–20.6] n=300
seat 519% [15.3–24.2] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.024. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
low-contention31% [25.7–36.1] n=300
small-country28% [23.2–33.3] n=300
random14% [10.2–18.0] n=300
blocker12% [8.8–16.2] n=300
concentrate10% [6.8–13.5] n=300
spread6% [3.8–9.3] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.093 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 4avg truce length (rounds) 99%truces betrayed 12%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 12% vs. a 17% fair share = a -4% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Structure

78edges 3.4avg degree 2–4degree range 2chokepoints 10diameter 42.3country flips/game

Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · 8c/46t #820502

46 territories · 8 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

31median rounds 100%decisive 39%snowball 31%comeback 0.015seat fairness 0.088persona fairness 0.8progression

Balance + fun score

82.3composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness85
persona fairness26
decisiveness100
length100
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

72%games with a swarm 97%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 97% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Turn order is fair (seat win-rate std-dev 0.015).
  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 31 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 39%, comeback 31%, 5.3 lead changes/game).
  • Country 5 is the easiest hold (bonus/border 1.67) but not dominant (1% of winners).

Weaknesses

  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.088); win rates: balanced 14%, countryal 19%, expander 15%, opportunist 10%, turtle 7%, warlord 34%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.088): the 'low-contention' opening wins 34% (fair share 17%).
  • Country 1 looks under-rewarded (bonus 2 for 4 borders; held by 9% of winners).

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 12540.509%
Country 22440.5012%
Country 31320.5015%
Country 43640.759%
Country 551231.671%
Country 63721.5014%
Country 72440.5011%
Country 82521.0011%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 3–12 (spread 9) · bonuses 1–5 · most defensible: Country 5 (5 bonus for 3 borders = 1.7 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
warlord34% [29.2–39.9] n=300
countryal19% [15.3–24.2] n=300
expander15% [11.7–19.8] n=300
balanced14% [10.8–18.8] n=300
opportunist10% [6.8–13.5] n=300
turtle7% [4.6–10.5] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.088.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 014% [10.2–18.0] n=300
seat 118% [14.1–22.7] n=300
seat 217% [13.2–21.7] n=300
seat 318% [14.1–22.7] n=300
seat 417% [13.2–21.7] n=300
seat 516% [12.6–20.9] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.015. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
low-contention34% [29.2–39.9] n=300
small-country19% [15.3–24.2] n=300
random15% [11.7–19.8] n=300
blocker14% [10.8–18.8] n=300
spread10% [6.8–13.5] n=300
concentrate7% [4.6–10.5] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.088 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 5avg truce length (rounds) 100%truces betrayed 12%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 12% vs. a 17% fair share = a -5% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Structure

82edges 3.6avg degree 2–5degree range 0chokepoints 10diameter 30.7country flips/game

Board data (JSON)

Show the raw board definition — countries, bonuses, territories, adjacency

The canonical vector format (loadable by graph.load_map / the map editor) — copy it to build a real, good-looking map from this board.

Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · 8c/46t #371898

46 territories · 8 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

27median rounds 100%decisive 36%snowball 34%comeback 0.007seat fairness 0.091persona fairness 1.0progression

Balance + fun score

81.4composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness93
persona fairness24
decisiveness100
length90
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

66%games with a swarm 98%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 98% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Turn order is fair (seat win-rate std-dev 0.007).
  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 27 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 36%, comeback 34%, 5.0 lead changes/game).
  • Country 4 is the easiest hold (bonus/border 3.00) but not dominant (2% of winners).

Weaknesses

  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.091); win rates: balanced 11%, countryal 31%, expander 12%, opportunist 12%, turtle 7%, warlord 27%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.091): the 'small-country' opening wins 31% (fair share 17%).

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 13740.752%
Country 23540.758%
Country 33540.7511%
Country 461423.002%
Country 52421.0021%
Country 62330.6722%
Country 72430.6714%
Country 82421.0022%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 3–14 (spread 11) · bonuses 2–6 · most defensible: Country 4 (6 bonus for 2 borders = 3.0 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
countryal31% [26.3–36.8] n=300
warlord27% [22.3–32.3] n=300
expander12% [8.8–16.2] n=300
opportunist12% [8.8–16.2] n=300
balanced11% [7.9–15.0] n=300
turtle7% [4.4–10.1] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.091.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 017% [13.2–21.7] n=300
seat 116% [12.6–20.9] n=300
seat 217% [12.9–21.3] n=300
seat 316% [12.0–20.2] n=300
seat 418% [14.1–22.7] n=300
seat 516% [12.6–20.9] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.007. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
small-country31% [26.3–36.8] n=300
low-contention27% [22.3–32.3] n=300
random12% [8.8–16.2] n=300
spread12% [8.8–16.2] n=300
blocker11% [7.9–15.0] n=300
concentrate7% [4.4–10.1] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.091 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 5avg truce length (rounds) 100%truces betrayed 15%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 15% vs. a 17% fair share = a -2% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Structure

80edges 3.5avg degree 2–5degree range 0chokepoints 9diameter 28.6country flips/game

Show the raw board definition — countries, bonuses, territories, adjacency

Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · 9c/47t #416815

47 territories · 9 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

30median rounds 100%decisive 38%snowball 31%comeback 0.011seat fairness 0.087persona fairness 1.1progression

Balance + fun score

83.3composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness89
persona fairness27
decisiveness100
length100
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

65%games with a swarm 97%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 97% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Turn order is fair (seat win-rate std-dev 0.011).
  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 30 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 38%, comeback 31%, 5.3 lead changes/game).
  • Country 3 is the easiest hold (bonus/border 1.50) but not dominant (4% of winners).
  • 1 chokepoint territory create defensible fronts.

Weaknesses

  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.087); win rates: balanced 13%, countryal 33%, expander 9%, opportunist 11%, turtle 10%, warlord 24%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.087): the 'small-country' opening wins 33% (fair share 17%).
  • Country 6 looks under-rewarded (bonus 1 for 3 borders; held by 23% of winners).

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 12530.677%
Country 22440.5016%
Country 33721.504%
Country 42621.0012%
Country 52621.008%
Country 61330.3323%
Country 72521.0028%
Country 83831.003%
Country 91330.3313%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 3–8 (spread 5) · bonuses 1–3 · most defensible: Country 3 (3 bonus for 2 borders = 1.5 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
countryal33% [27.9–38.5] n=300
warlord24% [19.2–28.8] n=300
balanced13% [9.4–16.9] n=300
opportunist11% [8.2–15.4] n=300
turtle10% [7.4–14.3] n=300
expander9% [6.3–12.8] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.087.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 016% [12.0–20.2] n=300
seat 115% [11.4–19.5] n=300
seat 217% [12.9–21.3] n=300
seat 317% [13.2–21.7] n=300
seat 418% [14.1–22.7] n=300
seat 518% [13.8–22.4] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.011. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
small-country33% [27.9–38.5] n=300
low-contention24% [19.2–28.8] n=300
blocker13% [9.4–16.9] n=300
spread11% [8.2–15.4] n=300
concentrate10% [7.4–14.3] n=300
random9% [6.3–12.8] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.087 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 5avg truce length (rounds) 99%truces betrayed 13%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 13% vs. a 17% fair share = a -4% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Structure

78edges 3.3avg degree 3–5degree range 1chokepoints 10diameter 39.3country flips/game
