Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · 9c/48t #373085

48 territories · 9 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

31median rounds 100%decisive 41%snowball 36%comeback 0.031seat fairness 0.094persona fairness 1.1progression

Balance + fun score

78.1composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness69
persona fairness22
decisiveness100
length100
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

68%games with a swarm 99%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 99% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 31 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 41%, comeback 36%, 5.7 lead changes/game).
  • Country 3 is the easiest hold (bonus/border 1.00) but not dominant (2% of winners).

Weaknesses

  • Turn-order advantage: seat 4 wins 22% (fair share 17%); seat fairness 0.031.
  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.094); win rates: balanced 13%, countryal 20%, expander 14%, opportunist 7%, turtle 10%, warlord 36%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.094): the 'low-contention' opening wins 36% (fair share 17%).
  • Country 6 looks under-rewarded (bonus 1 for 3 borders; held by 16% of winners).

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 11320.5023%
Country 23840.757%
Country 341041.002%
Country 42540.5011%
Country 52540.5011%
Country 61330.3316%
Country 71330.3316%
Country 83731.008%
Country 92440.5011%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 3–10 (spread 7) · bonuses 1–4 · most defensible: Country 3 (4 bonus for 4 borders = 1.0 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
warlord36% [30.5–41.2] n=300
countryal20% [16.2–25.2] n=300
expander14% [10.2–18.0] n=300
balanced13% [9.7–17.3] n=300
turtle10% [7.4–14.3] n=300
opportunist7% [4.6–10.5] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.094.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 012% [9.1–16.5] n=300
seat 118% [14.1–22.7] n=300
seat 217% [13.2–21.7] n=300
seat 315% [11.1–19.1] n=300
seat 422% [18.0–27.4] n=300
seat 516% [12.0–20.2] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.031. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
low-contention36% [30.5–41.2] n=300
small-country20% [16.2–25.2] n=300
random14% [10.2–18.0] n=300
blocker13% [9.7–17.3] n=300
concentrate10% [7.4–14.3] n=300
spread7% [4.6–10.5] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.094 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 6avg truce length (rounds) 99%truces betrayed 11%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 11% vs. a 17% fair share = a -6% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Structure

82edges 3.4avg degree 2–5degree range 0chokepoints 9diameter 40.7country flips/game

Show the raw board definition — countries, bonuses, territories, adjacency

Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · 9c/46t #864176

46 territories · 9 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

34median rounds 100%decisive 34%snowball 31%comeback 0.021seat fairness 0.113persona fairness 1.0progression

Balance + fun score

77.0composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness79
persona fairness6
decisiveness100
length100
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

68%games with a swarm 97%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 97% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Turn order is fair (seat win-rate std-dev 0.021).
  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 34 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 34%, comeback 31%, 6.4 lead changes/game).
  • Country 9 is the easiest hold (bonus/border 2.00) but not dominant (28% of winners).
  • 4 chokepoint territories create defensible fronts.

Weaknesses

  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.113); win rates: balanced 13%, countryal 31%, expander 12%, opportunist 7%, turtle 4%, warlord 33%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.113): the 'low-contention' opening wins 33% (fair share 17%).
  • Country 4 looks under-rewarded (bonus 2 for 4 borders; held by 7% of winners).

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 11311.0028%
Country 22530.6711%
Country 34960.672%
Country 42440.507%
Country 52440.509%
Country 63640.755%
Country 72530.676%
Country 82530.678%
Country 92512.0028%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 3–9 (spread 6) · bonuses 1–4 · most defensible: Country 9 (2 bonus for 1 border = 2.0 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
warlord33% [27.6–38.2] n=300
countryal31% [26.3–36.8] n=300
balanced13% [9.4–16.9] n=300
expander12% [9.1–16.5] n=300
opportunist7% [4.6–10.5] n=300
turtle4% [2.3–6.9] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.113.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 019% [14.7–23.5] n=300
seat 118% [14.1–22.7] n=300
seat 213% [9.9–17.6] n=300
seat 319% [15.0–23.8] n=300
seat 416% [12.6–20.9] n=300
seat 515% [11.1–19.1] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.021. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
low-contention33% [27.6–38.2] n=300
small-country31% [26.3–36.8] n=300
blocker13% [9.4–16.9] n=300
random12% [9.1–16.5] n=300
spread7% [4.6–10.5] n=300
concentrate4% [2.3–6.9] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.113 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 5avg truce length (rounds) 98%truces betrayed 13%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 13% vs. a 17% fair share = a -3% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · 9c/44t #554479

44 territories · 9 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

28median rounds 100%decisive 33%snowball 34%comeback 0.017seat fairness 0.105persona fairness 1.0progression

Balance + fun score

77.7composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness83
persona fairness12
decisiveness100
length93
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

65%games with a swarm 96%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 96% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Turn order is fair (seat win-rate std-dev 0.017).
  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 28 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 33%, comeback 34%, 5.4 lead changes/game).
  • Country 7 is the easiest hold (bonus/border 2.00) but not dominant (10% of winners).

Weaknesses

  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.105); win rates: balanced 16%, countryal 15%, expander 12%, opportunist 9%, turtle 9%, warlord 39%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.105): the 'low-contention' opening wins 39% (fair share 17%).
  • Country 6 looks under-rewarded (bonus 1 for 3 borders; held by 10% of winners).

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 11320.5013%
Country 21320.509%
Country 32521.0018%
Country 42550.405%
Country 52440.5019%
Country 61330.3310%
Country 741022.0010%
Country 83831.0010%
Country 91320.5010%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 3–10 (spread 7) · bonuses 1–4 · most defensible: Country 7 (4 bonus for 2 borders = 2.0 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
warlord39% [34.0–45.0] n=300
balanced16% [12.6–20.9] n=300
countryal15% [11.1–19.1] n=300
expander12% [9.1–16.5] n=300
opportunist9% [6.0–12.4] n=300
turtle9% [6.0–12.4] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.105.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 017% [13.2–21.7] n=300
seat 115% [11.7–19.8] n=300
seat 218% [13.8–22.4] n=300
seat 319% [15.0–23.8] n=300
seat 414% [10.2–18.0] n=300
seat 517% [13.5–22.0] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.017. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
low-contention39% [34.0–45.0] n=300
blocker16% [12.6–20.9] n=300
small-country15% [11.1–19.1] n=300
random12% [9.1–16.5] n=300
concentrate9% [6.0–12.4] n=300
spread9% [6.0–12.4] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.105 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 4avg truce length (rounds) 98%truces betrayed 13%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 13% vs. a 17% fair share = a -3% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Structure

72edges 3.3avg degree 2–5degree range 0chokepoints 10diameter 43.5country flips/game

Board data (JSON)

Show the raw board definition — countries, bonuses, territories, adjacency

The canonical vector format (loadable by graph.load_map / the map editor) — copy it to build a real, good-looking map from this board.

Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · 9c/46t #623767

46 territories · 9 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

24median rounds 100%decisive 42%snowball 28%comeback 0.018seat fairness 0.087persona fairness 1.4progression

Balance + fun score

78.3composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness82
persona fairness28
decisiveness100
length82
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

63%games with a swarm 98%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 98% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Turn order is fair (seat win-rate std-dev 0.018).
  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 42%, comeback 28%, 4.2 lead changes/game).
  • Country 7 is the easiest hold (bonus/border 2.00) but not dominant (2% of winners).

Weaknesses

  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.087); win rates: balanced 12%, countryal 26%, expander 12%, opportunist 8%, turtle 11%, warlord 31%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.087): the 'low-contention' opening wins 31% (fair share 17%).
  • Games end abruptly (median 24 rounds).
  • Country 4 looks under-rewarded (bonus 1 for 2 borders; held by 27% of winners).

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 12530.6722%
Country 22430.6712%
Country 33640.7510%
Country 41320.5027%
Country 52430.6721%
Country 62440.5011%
Country 741122.002%
Country 82421.0025%
Country 92540.505%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 3–11 (spread 8) · bonuses 1–4 · most defensible: Country 7 (4 bonus for 2 borders = 2.0 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
warlord31% [26.3–36.8] n=300
countryal26% [21.1–30.9] n=300
expander12% [9.1–16.5] n=300
balanced12% [8.8–16.2] n=300
turtle11% [7.9–15.0] n=300
opportunist8% [5.2–11.2] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.087.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 017% [12.9–21.3] n=300
seat 120% [16.2–25.2] n=300
seat 215% [11.1–19.1] n=300
seat 317% [12.9–21.3] n=300
seat 415% [11.7–19.8] n=300
seat 516% [12.6–20.9] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.018. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
low-contention31% [26.3–36.8] n=300
small-country26% [21.1–30.9] n=300
random12% [9.1–16.5] n=300
blocker12% [8.8–16.2] n=300
concentrate11% [7.9–15.0] n=300
spread8% [5.2–11.2] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.087 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 5avg truce length (rounds) 99%truces betrayed 18%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 18% vs. a 17% fair share = a +2% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Structure

73edges 3.2avg degree 2–5degree range 0chokepoints 11diameter 31.3country flips/game

Show the raw board definition — countries, bonuses, territories, adjacency

Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · 8c/46t #919287

46 territories · 8 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

34median rounds 100%decisive 35%snowball 36%comeback 0.024seat fairness 0.108persona fairness 0.9progression

Balance + fun score

77.2composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness76
persona fairness10
decisiveness100
length100
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

62%games with a swarm 97%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 97% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Turn order is fair (seat win-rate std-dev 0.024).
  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 34 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 35%, comeback 36%, 6.3 lead changes/game).
  • Country 7 is the easiest hold (bonus/border 2.00) but not dominant (7% of winners).

Weaknesses

  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.108); win rates: balanced 10%, countryal 30%, expander 14%, opportunist 7%, turtle 6%, warlord 33%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.108): the 'low-contention' opening wins 33% (fair share 17%).
  • Country 1 looks under-rewarded (bonus 2 for 4 borders; held by 14% of winners).

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 12440.5014%
Country 22330.6715%
Country 31320.5029%
Country 42440.506%
Country 53731.008%
Country 651131.671%
Country 74822.007%
Country 83631.006%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 3–11 (spread 8) · bonuses 1–5 · most defensible: Country 7 (4 bonus for 2 borders = 2.0 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
warlord33% [27.6–38.2] n=300
countryal30% [25.4–35.8] n=300
expander14% [10.2–18.0] n=300
balanced10% [7.1–13.9] n=300
opportunist7% [4.6–10.5] n=300
turtle6% [4.1–9.7] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.108.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 016% [12.3–20.6] n=300
seat 113% [9.4–16.9] n=300
seat 217% [13.2–21.7] n=300
seat 315% [11.7–19.8] n=300
seat 419% [15.0–23.8] n=300
seat 520% [15.9–24.9] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.024. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
low-contention33% [27.6–38.2] n=300
small-country30% [25.4–35.8] n=300
random14% [10.2–18.0] n=300
blocker10% [7.1–13.9] n=300
spread7% [4.6–10.5] n=300
concentrate6% [4.1–9.7] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.108 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 6avg truce length (rounds) 99%truces betrayed 12%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 12% vs. a 17% fair share = a -4% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Structure

83edges 3.6avg degree 2–5degree range 0chokepoints 8diameter 35.3country flips/game

Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · 8c/43t #768995

43 territories · 8 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

28median rounds 100%decisive 36%snowball 31%comeback 0.031seat fairness 0.084persona fairness 1.1progression

Balance + fun score

78.3composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness69
persona fairness30
decisiveness100
length92
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

62%games with a swarm 96%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 96% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 28 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 36%, comeback 31%, 4.8 lead changes/game).
  • Country 6 is the easiest hold (bonus/border 1.50) but not dominant (14% of winners).

Weaknesses

  • Turn-order advantage: seat 1 wins 20% (fair share 17%); seat fairness 0.031.
  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.084); win rates: balanced 10%, countryal 29%, expander 13%, opportunist 11%, turtle 9%, warlord 27%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.084): the 'small-country' opening wins 29% (fair share 17%).
  • Country 4 looks under-rewarded (bonus 1 for 2 borders; held by 29% of winners).

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 12530.675%
Country 24960.671%
Country 32430.6722%
Country 41320.5029%
Country 52530.6712%
Country 63821.5014%
Country 72540.505%
Country 82440.5017%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 3–9 (spread 6) · bonuses 1–4 · most defensible: Country 6 (3 bonus for 2 borders = 1.5 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
countryal29% [24.5–34.7] n=300
warlord27% [22.6–32.6] n=300
expander13% [9.9–17.6] n=300
opportunist11% [7.7–14.7] n=300
balanced10% [7.4–14.3] n=300
turtle9% [6.3–12.8] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.084.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 014% [10.2–18.0] n=300
seat 120% [16.2–25.2] n=300
seat 220% [15.9–24.9] n=300
seat 312% [8.8–16.2] n=300
seat 418% [13.8–22.4] n=300
seat 516% [12.6–20.9] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.031. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
small-country29% [24.5–34.7] n=300
low-contention27% [22.6–32.6] n=300
random13% [9.9–17.6] n=300
spread11% [7.7–14.7] n=300
blocker10% [7.4–14.3] n=300
concentrate9% [6.3–12.8] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.084 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 4avg truce length (rounds) 99%truces betrayed 21%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 21% vs. a 17% fair share = a +4% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Structure

74edges 3.4avg degree 2–4degree range 0chokepoints 10diameter 26.6country flips/game

Board data (JSON)

Show the raw board definition — countries, bonuses, territories, adjacency

The canonical vector format (loadable by graph.load_map / the map editor) — copy it to build a real, good-looking map from this board.

Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · 9c/51t #101869

51 territories · 9 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

28median rounds 100%decisive 39%snowball 30%comeback 0.020seat fairness 0.109persona fairness 1.0progression

Balance + fun score

76.9composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness80
persona fairness9
decisiveness100
length95
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

61%games with a swarm 96%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 96% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Turn order is fair (seat win-rate std-dev 0.020).
  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 28 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 39%, comeback 30%, 4.5 lead changes/game).
  • Country 1 is the easiest hold (bonus/border 2.00) but not dominant (7% of winners).

Weaknesses

  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.109); win rates: balanced 12%, countryal 39%, expander 9%, opportunist 8%, turtle 9%, warlord 22%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.109): the 'small-country' opening wins 39% (fair share 17%).
  • Country 5 looks under-rewarded (bonus 1 for 3 borders; held by 9% of winners).

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 14922.007%
Country 22550.406%
Country 32630.674%
Country 42530.6711%
Country 51330.339%
Country 61330.3311%
Country 72421.0036%
Country 82640.5010%
Country 941031.336%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 3–10 (spread 7) · bonuses 1–4 · most defensible: Country 1 (4 bonus for 2 borders = 2.0 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
countryal39% [33.3–44.3] n=300
warlord22% [17.7–27.0] n=300
balanced12% [9.1–16.5] n=300
expander9% [6.5–13.2] n=300
turtle9% [6.5–13.2] n=300
opportunist8% [5.7–12.0] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.109.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 017% [12.9–21.3] n=300
seat 120% [15.9–24.9] n=300
seat 218% [14.1–22.7] n=300
seat 317% [12.9–21.3] n=300
seat 415% [11.1–19.1] n=300
seat 514% [10.5–18.4] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.020. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
small-country39% [33.3–44.3] n=300
low-contention22% [17.7–27.0] n=300
blocker12% [9.1–16.5] n=300
concentrate9% [6.5–13.2] n=300
random9% [6.5–13.2] n=300
spread8% [5.7–12.0] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.109 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 5avg truce length (rounds) 99%truces betrayed 18%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 18% vs. a 17% fair share = a +1% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Structure

90edges 3.5avg degree 2–5degree range 0chokepoints 10diameter 33.2country flips/game

Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · Standard Risk World

42 territories · 6 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

31median rounds 100%decisive 46%snowball 22%comeback 0.018seat fairness 0.187persona fairness 0.9progression

Balance + fun score

76.5composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness82
persona fairness0
decisiveness100
length100
contestability100

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

67%games with a swarm 91%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 91% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Turn order is fair (seat win-rate std-dev 0.018).
  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 31 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 46%, comeback 22%, 5.3 lead changes/game).
  • 2 chokepoint territories create defensible fronts.

Weaknesses

  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.187); win rates: balanced 10%, countryal 58%, expander 7%, opportunist 11%, turtle 4%, warlord 11%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.187): the 'small-country' opening wins 58% (fair share 17%).
  • Australia is the most defensible-per-point (bonus 2 for 1 border(s); held by 52% of winners) — watch for over-valuation.

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
North America5931.675%
South America2421.0022%
Europe5741.251%
Africa3631.006%
Asia71251.400%
Australia2412.0052%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 4–12 (spread 8) · bonuses 2–7 · most defensible: Australia (2 bonus for 1 border = 2.0 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
countryal58% [52.3–63.4] n=300
warlord11% [7.9–15.0] n=300
opportunist11% [7.7–14.7] n=300
balanced10% [7.1–13.9] n=300
expander7% [4.4–10.1] n=300
turtle4% [2.1–6.4] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.187.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 019% [14.7–23.5] n=300
seat 119% [15.0–23.8] n=300
seat 215% [11.4–19.5] n=300
seat 317% [13.5–22.0] n=300
seat 415% [11.1–19.1] n=300
seat 515% [11.7–19.8] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.018. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
small-country58% [52.3–63.4] n=300
low-contention11% [7.9–15.0] n=300
spread11% [7.7–14.7] n=300
blocker10% [7.1–13.9] n=300
random7% [4.4–10.1] n=300
concentrate4% [2.1–6.4] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.187 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 5avg truce length (rounds) 97%truces betrayed 12%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 12% vs. a 17% fair share = a -4% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Structure

83edges 4.0avg degree 2–6degree range 2chokepoints 10diameter 12.1country flips/game

Generated by risk-lab · numbers are signals from these rules + AI, not final law. Run run_lab.py --sweep to rank this board against many territory/country permutations.

risk-lab · Classic

42 territories · 6 countries · 300 simulated 6-player games · expert AI, classic rules, no cards, random starts

Map (vectors)

Nodes = territories (colour = country), solid edges = intra-country adjacency, dashed = cross-country borders. Hover a node for its territory, country & bonus. Ringed nodes are chokepoints — capturing one splits the board in two.

Headline numbers

Hover the dotted-underlined stats, table headers, and play-styles (or any map dot) for a plain-English explanation.

32median rounds 100%decisive 45%snowball 18%comeback 0.016seat fairness 0.194persona fairness 0.8progression

Balance + fun score

76.0composite / 100
Component/100
seat fairness84
persona fairness0
decisiveness100
length100
contestability96

Each component is 0–100; the composite weights them 40% balance / 60% fun. Hover a component name for what it measures. A low bar shows exactly where the board falls short.

How games are won

0%elimination 100%70/70 dominance 0%round cap

A game ends when one player is the last standing (elimination), holds ≥70% of both the territories and the troops (dominance — Risk rarely grinds to a single survivor), or the round cap is hit and the leader wins (cap, non-decisive). Decisive rate = elimination + dominance.

Swarm (the board turns on a runaway)

62%games with a swarm 94%swarmed leader still won

Core rule: once a player holds ≥50% of the territories their allies break their truces and the table is free to gang up. Swarmed leader still won 94% — the lower it sits, the more a board lets the field punish a runaway rather than rubber-stamp the leader.

Strengths & weaknesses

Strengths

  • Turn order is fair (seat win-rate std-dev 0.016).
  • Games resolve cleanly (100% end decisively — elimination or 70/70 dominance; 100% by dominance).
  • Game length sits in a healthy band (median 32 rounds).
  • Lead is contestable (snowball 45%, comeback 18%, 5.3 lead changes/game).
  • 2 chokepoint territories create defensible fronts.

Weaknesses

  • Map rewards one play style (persona fairness 0.194); win rates: balanced 8%, countryal 60%, expander 6%, opportunist 9%, turtle 4%, warlord 13%.
  • Opening matters too much (setup fairness 0.194): the 'small-country' opening wins 60% (fair share 17%).
  • Country 6 is the most defensible-per-point (bonus 2 for 1 border(s); held by 48% of winners) — watch for over-valuation.

Countries

Country Bonus Size Borders Bonus / border Winner held @ mid
Country 15931.677%
Country 22421.0023%
Country 35741.251%
Country 43631.005%
Country 571251.400%
Country 62412.0048%

Bonus / border = reward per territory you must defend to keep the bonus (higher = more defensible, possibly over-valued). Winner held @ mid = share of game winners that already wholly owned this country at the midgame checkpoint — a springboard signal (end-of-game is uninformative since the winner owns the whole map).

Country sizes 4–12 (spread 8) · bonuses 2–7 · most defensible: Country 6 (2 bonus for 1 border = 2.0 per border). A real Risk board has varied countries — uniform sizes/bonuses play flat.

Balance detail

Win rates carry a 95% Wilson confidence interval (the whisker; the dashed line is the fair share). Two seats/styles whose intervals overlap are not reliably different on this many games — read gaps, not point estimates.

Win rate by play style

PersonaWin rate95% interval
countryal60% [54.0–65.1] n=300
warlord13% [9.4–16.9] n=300
opportunist9% [6.3–12.8] n=300
balanced8% [5.7–12.0] n=300
expander6% [3.8–9.3] n=300
turtle4% [2.5–7.3] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.194.

Win rate by turn order

SeatWin rate95% interval
seat 016% [12.0–20.2] n=300
seat 119% [15.0–23.8] n=300
seat 216% [12.6–20.9] n=300
seat 317% [13.5–22.0] n=300
seat 418% [13.8–22.4] n=300
seat 514% [10.5–18.4] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Fairness (std-dev) = 0.016. This per-seat spread is its own fairness axis — turn order shouldn't decide the game.

Leader-penalty curve

Win rate (blue) vs each seat's early (25%-mark) territory share, with 95% Wilson whiskers and the fair-share reference (orange dashes). A curve that climbs straight up = a snowball board where an early lead just compounds; a curve that flattens or dips once a seat is clearly ahead = a healthy board where the early leader draws focus-fire and the lead is losable.

Openings (initial-reinforcement strategy)

OpeningWin rate95% interval
small-country60% [54.0–65.1] n=300
low-contention13% [9.4–16.9] n=300
spread9% [6.3–12.8] n=300
blocker8% [5.7–12.0] n=300
random6% [3.8–9.3] n=300
concentrate4% [2.5–7.3] n=300

Fair share = 17%. Setup fairness (std-dev) = 0.194 — how much the way a seat claims and stacks (concentrate / small-country / spread / …) decides the game. Low = the board is opening-agnostic; high = it rewards one opening regardless of play.

Diplomacy (truces & betrayal)

100%games with a truce 4avg truce length (rounds) 98%truces betrayed 20%betrayer win rate

A truce is a non-aggression pact formed during play (never before placing). Betrayer win rate 20% vs. a 17% fair share = a +3% betrayal premium — whether turning on a partner is rewarded on this board.

Structure

83edges 4.0avg degree 2–6degree range 2chokepoints 10diameter 12.6country flips/game
